quote: one of the things you said upfront immediately gives me pause. You said that, power would be, provided by you guys primarily
stewart_read: This is the root-cause moment of the call's scope-creep spiral. Jody flags a specific concern about grid connectivity — she heard Carter's explanation as implying Dominion would be disconnected. Carter's response is to clarify and engage the technical premise rather than acknowledge and bridge. Strategic hypothesis: Carter is building rapport by meeting Jody on her technical level before redirecting. Reactive hypothesis: Carter has no redirect instinct and simply follows the customer's lead. The reactive read holds — no redirect attempt follows for the next four-plus minutes, and Carter ends up doing live panel arithmetic.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter is deliberately engaging Jody's technical concern to build credibility before pivoting to the verify script.
coaching_implication: Show Carter this timestamp. The correct move here is: 'That's a fair concern — and it's exactly the kind of thing our specialist will walk through with you on the design call. They'll show you your specific numbers. My job is just to see if you qualify.' One sentence. The call never needed to go where it went.

stewart_read: Carter is doing live panel-count arithmetic on a call where his stated job is scheduling. He has already admitted at 04:23 that he doesn't know the answer ('I'm not too sure. I just dealt with the scheduling side of things') — and then continues calculating anyway. This is scope creep compounded by inaccuracy: Carter later revises his own count ('It's only nine panels. Yeah. I'm not doing that right') at 07:06. The reactive read is unambiguous — there is no strategic setup here.
coaching_implication: Ask Carter: what happens when the closer's actual satellite design produces a different panel count than what you told her? Who owns that conversation? The setter who does the closer's job badly creates a trust problem the closer has to clean up before they can sell. Use schema.setter_scope_discipline's universal escape hatch in the 1-on-1: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer.'

quote: I'd love to just get your utility bill so we know how many panels you would need, and then just send it over to my design team.
stewart_read: Carter's first redirect to the verify/bill-collection path comes at 07:14 — more than seven minutes into the call. This is a late recovery, not a planned move. The redirect itself is clean and lands without resistance, which is worth acknowledging. But the timing means Carter spent the entire opening of the call in scope-creep territory before attempting the move that should have come at minute two.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter waited until he had established enough technical credibility with Jody to make the redirect land. The late timing was deliberate.
coaching_implication: The redirect worked — use that as the coaching anchor. 'This line at 07:26 is good. What would have happened if you'd said it at 02:43 instead?' The goal is to help Carter see that the redirect was available much earlier and that engaging the technical questions first cost him five minutes and created inaccurate panel counts the closer now has to manage.

quote: Okay. I mean, we could just build that off square footage as well.
stewart_read: When Jody reveals she hasn't moved in yet and won't have a utility bill for two months, Carter pivots immediately to square footage as a design input. This is a genuine positive recovery — Carter finds a workable path around a qualification gap without losing the booking. The move is reactive in origin (he didn't anticipate the pre-move situation) but the execution is clean and keeps the call moving.
coaching_implication: Name this explicitly in the 1-on-1 as something Carter did well. 'When the bill path closed, you found another way in. That's the right instinct.' Then pair it with the scope-creep coaching: the same problem-solving energy that found the square-footage pivot could have found the specialist-redirect at 02:43.

quote: one of the main requirements for getting solar is having a credit score above 650. Is that gonna be an issue for you?
stewart_read: Carter states the credit threshold as 650. The schema schema records 670 as the threshold, with a noted drift instance from SESSION18_fcbf33c1 where a different rep also stated 650. This is a schema-drift flag, not a coaching failure per se — but it is the second documented instance of a rep stating 650 instead of 670. Either the active threshold has changed and the schema hasn't been updated, or this is a floor-wide drift that needs correction.
coaching_implication: Flag for manager to resolve with Kenny: is the active threshold 650 or 670? This is the second rep on record stating 650. If 670 is correct, this is a floor-wide calibration issue, not an individual coaching moment. If 650 is the current policy, update the schema. Do not coach Carter on this until the threshold is confirmed.

quote: So here's where my issue is, and we'll just cut to it. In the city of Richmond, I don't know if you're aware, in the zip code or whatever, on a rubber membrane roof, they want a four foot setback from all the edges of the roof.
stewart_read: Jody's disclosure of a rubber membrane roof with a 4-foot setback requirement is a qualification signal the closer needs — but it is also an enthusiasm signal. Jody has researched local code requirements for her specific roof type before this call. That level of pre-call research indicates a customer who is genuinely evaluating solar, not just browsing. Carter engages the technical constraint but never acknowledges the research effort or uses it to anchor Jody's motivation. The roof type itself (rubber membrane) is a verify item that should be captured and flagged for the closer.
coaching_implication: Two things for the manager to surface: (1) Carter should have captured 'rubber membrane roof' as a verify item and flagged it for the closer — it may affect installation approach. (2) The research Jody has done is a buying signal. A line like 'You've clearly done your homework on this — that's exactly the kind of detail our specialist will factor into your design' acknowledges the effort and bridges to the appointment without engaging the technical constraint.

quote: What since you're not in the home right now, I'll just set up a time for a phone call, and we can just go over it super quick, see if it even would make sense for your home.
stewart_read: Carter books the appointment but completes only two of the five required button-up elements: phone_appointment_specified (implicit in 'phone call') and text_followup_promise (10:17). Missing: one_hour_block_stated, no_driving_at_computer, is_that_fair_handshake. The 'see if it even would make sense for your home' framing is also soft — it leaves the appointment's value conditional on a feasibility question Jody already has doubts about. The closer walks in without a verbal handshake and without a customer who has been anchored to the appointment's purpose.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter kept the close soft intentionally to avoid pressure on a customer who has expressed feasibility concerns.
coaching_implication: Walk Carter through the button-up checklist against this call. Three elements missing. Focus especially on is_that_fair_handshake — the verbal handshake is the single highest-leverage no-show reducer in the protocol. 'Is that fair?' takes three seconds and Carter skipped it. Also flag the 'see if it even would make sense' framing: the appointment should be anchored to what Jody will learn, not to whether it makes sense — that framing invites a pre-appointment cancellation.

quote: I mean, we probably wouldn't do that with you if there's only half of your needs.
stewart_read: Carter's response to Jody's 50%-of-needs benchmark is to implicitly validate the concern as a potential disqualifier ('we probably wouldn't do that with you'). This is a scope-creep move in the wrong direction — Carter is pre-qualifying the feasibility of the install rather than bridging to the specialist. The correct move is to treat the 50% figure as the specialist's input, not the setter's judgment. Strategic hypothesis: Carter is being honest to build trust. Reactive hypothesis: Carter has no redirect and is following Jody's framing. The reactive read holds — no bridge follows.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter is being transparent about Ion's standards to build credibility with a research-primed customer.
coaching_implication: This line is the earliest point where Carter could have used the universal escape hatch. 'Whether 50% works for your situation is exactly what our specialist will calculate — they'll show you the actual numbers for your roof. My job is just to see if you qualify.' Instead Carter accepted Jody's framing and the call went technical for the next four minutes.
